questions.question: A $2.0\text{ kg}$ mass is acted upon by a resultant force of $6.0\text{ N}$. Calculate the acceleration of the mass.
questions.explanation:
Using $F = ma$, acceleration $a = F / m = 6.0 / 2.0 = 3.0\text{ m/s}^2$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying the mass and force to get $12.0$ instead of dividing.

questions.question: An object of mass $500\text{ g}$ accelerates at $2.0\text{ m/s}^2$. What is the resultant force acting on it?
questions.explanation:
First, convert mass to kg: $500\text{ g} = 0.5\text{ kg}$. Then $F = ma = 0.5 \times 2.0 = 1.0\text{ N}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the mass in grams ($500 \times 2 = 1000\text{ N}$) without converting to SI units.

questions.question: A constant force of $12\text{ N}$ is applied to a box of mass $4.0\text{ kg}$ on a rough floor. If the box accelerates at $2.0\text{ m/s}^2$, what is the frictional force?
questions.explanation:
Resultant force $F_{net} = ma = 4.0 \times 2.0 = 8.0\text{ N}$. Since $F_{net} = F_{applied} - F_{friction}$, then $8 = 12 - F_{friction}$, so $F_{friction} = 4.0\text{ N}$. 

**Common mistake:** Identifying the acceleration ($2.0$) or the resultant force ($8.0$) as the frictional force.

questions.question: A resultant force of $4\text{ N}$ causes an object to accelerate at $2\text{ m/s}^2$. What force is needed to make the same object accelerate at $3\text{ m/s}^2$?
questions.explanation:
Mass of object $m = F / a = 4 / 2 = 2\text{ kg}$. New force required $F = ma = 2 \times 3 = 6\text{ N}$. 

**Common mistake:** Adding $1\text{ N}$ to the force because the acceleration increased by $1$.

questions.question: Which of these is a correct statement about friction?
options: Friction can cause heating in moving parts
There is no friction in liquids or gases
Friction only acts on objects that are moving
Friction always acts in the same direction as the applied force
questions.explanation:
Friction is a force that opposes motion and can generate thermal energy (heating). 

**Common mistake:** Thinking fluids (liquids/gases) don't have friction; they experience drag and viscosity.
